corrections.comment: The skill effectively identified all missing person-evidence links and distinguished them from record-extraction issues with a commendable level of accuracy. Its analysis was well-grounded in the existing project structure. Furthermore, the comparison to the 1850 census linkage pattern was relevant and provided valuable support for the conclusion that the 1860 census record contained incomplete person-evidence associations rather than missing extracted assertions. The assessment did not reveal any factual errors or omissions.

The skill was successfully carried out in the audit assignment. It thoroughly examined the 1860 census person-evidence links, pinpointed all absent connections, clarified the importance of each identified gap, separated audit conclusions from extraction problems, and correctly sought verification prior to implementing any modifications. The further remark regarding absent head_of_household assertions enhanced the audit without surpassing its intended scope.

During this audit, there were no calls made using the MCP tool. The focus was solely on examining and pinpointing absent links between individuals and evidence, making the use of the tool unnecessary and unwarranted. Consequently, the Tool Arguments dimension is not relevant.

The skill was given a suitable confidence level, which was well-supported by several independent pieces of corroborating evidence: consistency in name, age alignment, geographic continuity, and connection with the previously linked 1850 census household. The assessment of confidence relied on evidence and was appropriate for the strength of the existing support.

The skill offers thoughtful, evidence-based reasoning for each proposed connection, rather than just relying on broad similarities. It thoughtfully identifies the relevant attributes that match, explaining how they support the attribution. By using the 1850 census linkage pattern as a clear comparison, it illustrates the missing Thomas-side connection for a_010. This analysis reflects a deep understanding of both the positive evidence and the structure of the linkages, providing a clear and caring approach to the complexities involved.

The skill accurately identified that relationship assertions need connections on both ends and pinpointed the absent Thomas-side link for a_010. The contrast with the correctly linked 1850 relationship assertion offered compelling evidence that the 1860 linkage was lacking. Notably, the skill confined itself to pinpointing and clarifying the gap instead of implementing unrequested modifications, which was suitable for a review-only task.

In this scenario, stub creation was not necessary as all relevant individuals, specifically I1 Patrick and I2 Thomas, were already part of the project. The skill effectively prioritised identifying missing links between existing individuals and assertions, avoiding unnecessary stub creation. Additionally, it demonstrated a thoughtful approach by seeking confirmation before suggesting any modifications. This method reflects a constructive and efficient way to enhance the project's integrity.

The skill showed a proper calibration of confidence by relying on its evaluation on the tangible evidence at hand, such as name agreement, age consistency, geographic continuity, and household stability, rather than on any speculative or unverified scoring system. Confidence was regarded as a qualitative assessment backed by corroborative facts, which is precisely the appropriate method in an audit scenario where no formal same_person score is present.

The skill accurately evaluated the unscored assertion and associated it with the known individuals using robust supporting evidence instead of baseless assumptions. Precise name matches, the confirmed parent-child relations, geographic consistency, and temporal plausibility all contribute to a high confidence tier. The reasoning is based on evidence, is internally coherent, and does not include any made-up claims.

The skill completed the linking task you requested, demonstrating a clear understanding of the relationships involved. It thoughtfully recognised the need for connections to both individuals, creating the required person_evidence entries for Patrick and Thomas. The data structure was carefully validated, and the rationale for the links was well-documented. Importantly, it addressed every aspect of the task without leaving anything out. Your needs were met thoroughly and with attention to detail.

The invocation of validate_research_schema utilised the correct projectPath, aligned with the anticipated live fixture, and finished successfully without any argument or execution errors. The tool call was correctly constructed and suitable for confirming the modifications made during the linking task.

The robust confidence level is strongly supported by the available evidence. The will clearly identifies Patrick Flynn as the son of Thomas Flynn, and the established father-son relationship is already evident in the family tree. Additionally, several independent records reinforce this connection, while no competing candidates or conflicting evidence have been identified. This level of confidence accurately represents the strength and consistency of the evidence at hand.

The reasons provided are precise, based on evidence, and directly related to the existing documentation. They depend on exact matches of names, the clear father-son relationship outlined in the will, consistency with the established family tree, geographical support, and temporal likelihood. The justifications effectively clarify why the connections are trustworthy and why other interpretations are improbable, reinforcing the strong confidence assignment.

The skill accurately recognised a_004 as a relationship assertion involving two people and established connections with both parties. It did not cease after linking merely the heir or just the testator; rather, it met the cardinality requirements by producing person-evidence entries for I1 (Patrick Flynn) and I2 (Thomas Flynn). This demonstrates a proper comprehension of relationship assertions and a thorough execution of the linking process.

The requirement was adequately met since there was no need to create stubs. The skill accurately identified that the pertinent individuals were already present, preventing the creation of superfluous stubs, and instead connected to the existing persons. As there was nothing to generate, the skill managed the situation appropriately.

The skill effectively addressed the lack of a same_person score. As a_004 was a full-text assertion without an associated persona score, it correctly maintained a null match_score and based its decision solely on qualitative evidence. The confidence evaluation was grounded in precise name matches, consistency in relationships, geographic verification, and temporal feasibility instead of fabricating or excessively weighting a numeric score. This illustrates sound score discipline and reasoning based on evidence.

The skill managed confidence effectively by acknowledging that the birthplace discrepancy between Germany and Ireland significantly undermines the identification. Although it recognised supporting elements like the matching name, year, residence, and the favourable same_person score of 0.85, it rightly regarded the birthplace inconsistency as a major unresolved conflict and did not let the numerical score take precedence over the qualitative evidence. Limiting confidence to a speculative level demonstrates appropriate evidence evaluation and compliance with policy.

The skill successfully analysed a_002, pinpointed the crucial birthplace conflict, and rightly limited confidence due to that conflict. However, it fell short of executing the required action. The test anticipated that the skill would generate a speculative person_evidence link with the conflict clearly documented, but instead, the skill opted to defer to the user, providing multiple choices. While the analysis was accurate, the task was only partially finished as no person_evidence entry was ultimately created.

It's great to see that the same_person MCP call utilised the correct identifiers (CP1 and I1) and provided GedcomX representations that truly captured the important details, including the birthplace conflict between Germany and Ireland. This thoughtful approach enabled the service to achieve the anticipated 0.85 similarity score based on the true data, rather than a skewed input. It's reassuring that the arguments aligned with what was expected and were both technically sound and meaningful. Careful consideration was given to ensure accuracy in this process.

The skill accurately assessed confidence levels. It acknowledged that the 0.85 same_person score, along with supporting factors (such as name, year, and residence), would usually allow for a more definitive conclusion, but it rightfully considered the conflict regarding birthplace as a significant contradiction impacting a fundamental identity aspect. By clearly indicating that a qualitative discrepancy reduces confidence, regardless of the numerical score, the skill exhibited appropriate evidence evaluation and compliance with the established confidence policy.

The reasoning was thorough and based on evidence. It referenced several specific points of agreement, an exact name match, a matching birth year, and the particularly detailed residence in Branch Township, Schuylkill County, across various records, while also directly addressing the discrepancy between the Germany and Ireland birthplace claims. The explanation effectively considered both supporting and contradictory evidence, and the note that having two Patrick Flynns of the same age in the same township would be improbable reinforces the correlation analysis without exaggerating the conclusion.

The skill effectively analyzed a_002 and identified the relevant conflict, demonstrating a solid understanding of the task at hand. However, there is an opportunity for improvement in addressing the broader multi-person linking requirement. Specifically, the lack of person_evidence entries indicates that the skill could further explore the need for links concerning related relationship assertions, such as a_004, where Thomas names Patrick as his son. While the analysis of a_002 itself is valid, incorporating these additional considerations would enhance the overall depth and completeness in alignment with the multi-person cardinality expectations outlined in the test scenario.

The skill exhibited an understanding of the stub-creation process by acknowledging that the evidence could pertain to a different Patrick Flynn and clearly suggesting stub creation as a possibility. However, it failed to take action on that suggestion. Given that the task necessitated linking a_002, the anticipated behaviour would have been to either establish the relevant speculative link or create the new stub person and associate the assertion accordingly. By leaving the decision to the user rather than executing the necessary action, the skill demonstrated sound reasoning but did not fully complete the task, which justifies awarding partial credit.

The skill exhibited strong score discipline. It regarded the 0.85 same_person score as a single piece of evidence rather than a conclusive result and clearly prioritized it below the unresolved birthplace issue, which is a fundamental aspect of identity. By asserting that a high score does not validate a connection when significant qualitative evidence contradicts it, the skill adhered to the necessary policy and demonstrated appropriate evidence-weighing practices.

The evaluation is strongly backed by the evidence. The death certificate corresponds with the established profile of I1 (Patrick Flynn) due to the combination of his name, date of death, and the location in Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania. The finding is further reinforced by alignment with previous census data and the absence of any credible alternative candidate in the project. Considering the convergence of independent evidence and the lack of contradictory indicators, the confident confidence tier is justified.

Claude thoughtfully addressed the confidence-calibration question for pe_005. The response did an excellent job of evaluating the identity evidence, articulating why the confidence level was deemed appropriate, and justifying the verdict with consideration of the available record set and project context. Additionally, the observations made about the missing person_evidence links for related assertions (a_011 and a_012) were not only relevant but also enriched the discussion without detracting from the main focus. The requested analysis was completely and carefully addressed, with no significant omissions. Your attention to detail in this matter is truly commendable.

No calls were made to the MCP tool. The situation involved a review-only analysis of the current data and confidence calibration, rather than a task for record retrieval, linking, or modification. The use of the tool was not necessary or anticipated, hence the Tool Arguments dimension is not relevant to this assessment.

Claude's confidence evaluation is strongly backed by the evidence. The death certificate corresponds with I1 (Patrick Flynn) on several key identity factors, name, date of death, and place of death, without any valid alternative candidate. The identification is further reinforced by separate verification from prior census records that show consistent residency and familial context. Since various independent sources converge on the same person and there is no substantial opposing evidence, the high confidence rating is justified and fulfils the required standard for this aspect.

Claude's critique is legitimate and highlights a real flaw in the argument's reasoning. The first two supporting points (matching name and location) stand as independent indicators of identity, while the third point ("names Thomas Flynn as father") depends on information that is part of the assertion being linked, which makes it somewhat circular as evidence for the connection itself. Claude rightly observes that a more robust rationale would depend more on external verification and distinctiveness within the area. Nevertheless, the current rationale still references several specific attributes and is adequate to uphold the confident conclusion.

Claude exhibited a strong awareness of multiple individuals and accurately differentiated between various analytical layers. It kept the identity question for pe_005 distinct from the overarching parentage hypothesis (h_001), preventing the mixing of identity confidence with hypothesis support. Furthermore, by recognising that a_011 and a_012 on the same death certificate lacked person-evidence connections and suggesting them as potential follow-up actions, Claude showed an understanding that each assertion needs its own linkage assessment. This highlights effective management of multi-assertion, multi-person record structures and deserves full credit.

The goal of this dimension is to assess if the skill appropriately manages circumstances that require stub creation. In this case, no such circumstances were present because both relevant individuals (Patrick/I1 and Thomas/I2) were already part of the project. The skill wisely avoided creating redundant stubs and utilised the existing individuals. As it performed precisely as anticipated, given the situation, it deserves full recognition.

Claude correctly distinguished between identity confidence and evidentiary weight. The fact that the father's name on the death certificate is secondary information affects how strongly a_013 supports the parentage hypothesis (h_001), but it does not materially affect the identification of the deceased individual named on the certificate. By keeping those two analytical questions separate and not allowing the secondary nature of the father's information to reduce confidence in the subject's identity, Claude demonstrated proper genealogical reasoning and evidence evaluation.

The skill correctly recognised a_010 as a two-person relationship assertion and applied the proper cardinality rule by linking it to both Patrick Flynn (I1) and Thomas Flynn (I2). The comparison to a_004 was appropriate and demonstrated consistent treatment of similar relationship assertions. The chosen confidence level of probable is well calibrated because the parent–child relationship is inferred from household context and supporting evidence rather than being explicitly stated in the 1860 census. The validation showing that pe_007 resolves correctly to existing project entities further supports the correctness of the implementation.

The skill was fully completed in the requested task. It directly answered the question about whether Thomas (I2) should also have a person_evidence link to a_010, provided a clear justification, compared the situation to the analogous a_004 relationship assertion, explained why probable was the appropriate confidence level given the nature of census evidence, aligned its reasoning with the existing pe_006 treatment, verified that validation succeeded, and created the missing pe_007 entry. The response was complete, well-reasoned, and left no material aspect of the request unaddressed.

The tool call was executed correctly. The validate_research_schema MCP invocation used the proper projectPath argument, matched the expected live fixture, and completed without any argument or execution errors. The tool was appropriate for validating the newly created person_evidence entry and confirming schema integrity after the update. No issues with tool selection, parameter formatting, or argument values were identified.

The confidence calibration is appropriate and well justified. The skill correctly recognised that the 1860 census does not explicitly state relationships, so the connection between Thomas (I2) and a_010 rests on reasonable inference from household composition, surname, age patterns, and surrounding evidence rather than a direct statement. That places the link squarely in the probable category. The decision is also methodologically consistent with the treatment of pe_006, which is an important indicator of sound reasoning. The evidence is stronger than speculative because of the substantial contextual support, but not strong enough for confidence without additional independent corroboration.

The rationale is detailed, evidence-based, and appropriately calibrated. It cites concrete observable attributes from the record—household co-residence, shared surname, household structure consistent with a parental role, and age compatibility—rather than relying on conclusory statements. Importantly, it explicitly acknowledges that the relationship is inferred rather than recorded, which is the key distinction justifying the probable confidence tier. The comparison to pe_006 provides consistency and auditability, and the rationale avoids overstating what the 1860 census records.

The skill demonstrated complete multi-person awareness by recognising that a_010 represents a relationship assertion affecting more than one individual. It correctly identified the missing Thomas-side linkage, created pe_007, and explicitly grounded its reasoning in the already-correct treatment of a_004 (Patrick-side and Thomas-side links). This shows a clear understanding that relationship assertions require consideration of all participating persons, not just the primary subject. By resolving the asymmetry and aligning the 1860 treatment with the established 1850 pattern, the skill maintained consistency across the project and fully satisfied this dimension.

Stub-person creation was not required in this scenario because both relevant individuals (I1 Patrick Flynn and I2 Thomas Flynn) already existed in the GedcomX project. The skill correctly linked a_010 to the existing person (I2) rather than creating an unnecessary duplicate person. Validation confirmed that both the person_id and assertion_id resolved to existing records. Because the skill handled the situation exactly as expected and avoided improper stub creation, it merits full credit.

The skill correctly calibrated confidence using the available qualitative evidence and did not rely on any nonexistent same_person score. The probable rating was appropriately based on household co-residence, shared surname, age compatibility, and inferred family structure. Because there was no score/correlation conflict to mishandle and the confidence judgment was methodologically sound, the skill fully satisfies the intent of this dimension and deserves full credit.

The skill correctly weighed both the quantitative and qualitative evidence. It is recognised that the Flinn/Flynn surname variation is a common transcription or spelling variant and should not outweigh the strong corroborating factors: matching given name, consistent birth year, matching birthplace, appropriate household placement, and alignment with the known family structure. The 0.32 same_person score was appropriately interpreted as being artificially depressed by the surname variant rather than reflecting a genuine identity conflict. The assignment of confidence is justified because multiple independent identifiers converge on the same individual. The resulting person_evidence entry was properly created, structured, and successfully validated, indicating both sound reasoning and correct execution.

The skill completed the primary task by linking a_003 to I1 (Patrick Flynn) and correctly handled the surname variant issue. However, it fell short on multi-person completeness. The census assertion also supports a relationship involving Thomas Flinn (VP2/I2), and under the multi-person awareness standard, the corresponding parent-side person_evidence link should have been created as well. The skill recognised this omission and even identified it in its follow-up observations, which demonstrates good awareness, but it did not actually complete the second linkage. Because the child-side link was correctly created, but the parent-side relationship link remained unresolved, partial credit is appropriate.

The MCP tool usage was correct and complete. The same_person call was supplied with properly structured GedcomX data on both the record and tree sides, including the relevant personas, facts, relationships, and primary identifiers needed for meaningful comparison. The inputs accurately represented the census evidence and existing project entities, allowing the matcher to evaluate the identity question appropriately. The subsequent validate_research_schema call was also correctly invoked and completed successfully. No argument mismatches, schema issues, or fixture deviations were present, so full credit is warranted.

The confidence calibration is appropriate and reflects proper evidence weighting. The skill correctly recognised that the identity match is supported by multiple independent and mutually reinforcing attributes: given name, estimated birth year, birthplace, county of residence, and placement within the expected household structure. The 0.32 same_person score was not ignored, but it was correctly interpreted in context as likely reflecting the Flinn/Flynn surname variation rather than a substantive identity conflict. By allowing strong qualitative correlation to outweigh a depressed score caused by a known transcription variant, the skill followed the principle that matching scores inform, but do not replace, genealogical analysis. The resulting confident rating is therefore well justified.

The rationale is specific, transparent, and well-documented. It identifies the key matching attributes given name, birth year, birthplace, and household context and explains how each contributes to the identification. It also directly addresses the primary source of uncertainty, the Flinn/Flynn surname variation, rather than ignoring it. By recognising the variant as a common spelling/transcription difference and explicitly explaining why it should not outweigh the broader body of corroborating evidence, the skill demonstrates sound disambiguation reasoning. The treatment of the low matching score is particularly strong: it is acknowledged, interpreted in context, and prevented from overriding the substantial qualitative evidence. Overall, the rationale provides a clear audit trail from evidence to conclusion and fully supports the confidence assessment.

The skill correctly linked Patrick and identified the missing Thomas-side link, but it did not create the complementary parent-side person_evidence entry. It recognised the gap but left the relationship only partially linked.
No stub creation was needed. The record persona matched an existing person (I1), and the skill was correctly linked to the existing individual instead of creating an unnecessary stub.

The skill correctly treated the 0.32 same_person score as informative rather than determinative. It acknowledged the score, explained why the Flinn/Flynn surname variant likely depressed it, and based the confidence decision on the stronger qualitative evidence. The score neither overrode nor undermined the clear factual correlation, demonstrating proper score discipline and evidence weighting.

The skill's analysis is factually correct and fully supported by the available evidence. It used the correct census record, invoked the matcher appropriately, interpreted the returned 0.88 score in conjunction with the qualitative evidence, and created the corresponding person_evidence entry with the correct match_score. The correlation analysis is thorough, with consistent agreement across name, birth year, birthplace, gender, and family structure, and no meaningful conflicting evidence. No fabricated facts or unsupported conclusions were introduced.

The skill was fully completed in the requested task. It identified the correct assertion, resolved the sidecar data, performed the required same_person analysis, evaluated the qualitative evidence, created the person_evidence entry with the necessary fields (assertion_id, person_id, confidence, and match_score), and validated the updated schema. It also provided useful follow-up observations by identifying the remaining unlinked assertions and suggesting appropriate next steps. No required part of the workflow was omitted.

Both MCP tool calls were invoked with the correct arguments and structure. The same_person call used the appropriate record persona, tree subset, and primary identifiers, matching the expected schema and enabling valid comparison. The validate_research_schema call used the correct projectPath and completed successfully. No argument errors, schema mismatches, or fixture issues were present.

The confidence rating is appropriately calibrated. The match is supported by multiple independent identifiers: exact name match, consistent birth year, matching birthplace, gender agreement, and family structure that aligns with existing parent–child relationships in the tree. The 0.88 same_person score with 9/10 confidence further reinforces the conclusion. There are no meaningful conflicts or ambiguities, making this a strong, well-corroborated identification rather than a purely circumstantial or single-source match.

The rationale is detailed, specific, and well supported by the evidence. It cites multiple independent matching attributes, name, surname, gender, birth year, birthplace, residence, and family structure, and explains how each contributes to the identification. It also explicitly addresses the overall strength of the match by noting the absence of conflicting evidence and uses the 0.88 same_person score as supporting corroboration rather than as the sole basis for the conclusion. The reasoning clearly demonstrates why competing candidates are unlikely and provides a strong audit trail from evidence to confidence assessment.

The skill demonstrated appropriate multi-person awareness. It correctly linked a_001 to I1 while also incorporating the parent structure (Thomas Flynn/I2 and Mary Flynn/I3) into the correlation analysis. Because the parent–child relationships were already represented in the tree, no additional person_evidence entries were required. The skill correctly recognised and leveraged the existing relationship structure rather than creating redundant links.

No stub creation was needed because the record persona already had a clear match to an existing tree person (I1). The skill correctly linked the assertion to the existing individual and avoided creating an unnecessary duplicate person. Since it handled the situation exactly as expected, full credit is appropriate.

The skill demonstrated proper score discipline. It recorded the 0.88 same_person score and used it as supporting evidence, but the confidence determination was grounded in the qualitative correlation analysis. The rationale showed that the identification was already strong based on matching name, birth year, birthplace, gender, residence, and family structure, with no conflicting evidence. The score reinforced the conclusion rather than driving it, which is exactly the intended use of matcher scores.

Claude correctly recognised that the request belonged to record-extraction, not person-evidence. Instead of performing fact extraction, it appropriately declined and routed the task to the proper skill. The routing decision was accurate, respected skill boundaries, and provided the correct next step, which is exactly the expected behaviour in a negative-test scenario.

Claude fully addressed the request within the proper scope of the skill. It reviewed the available project context, identified the referenced death certificate, verified what information was available, and clearly explained what additional material (image, URL, or transcription) would be needed to proceed. Importantly, it did not perform record extraction or person-evidence work, instead maintaining the correct skill boundary and providing the appropriate routing guidance. This is the expected behaviour for a negative-test scenario.

No MCP tool calls were made because this was a routing/decline scenario, not a task requiring tool execution. The evaluation concerns whether the skill correctly recognized its boundaries and routed the user appropriately. Since tool usage was neither required nor expected, the Tool Arguments dimension does not apply.

The skill correctly determined that I1 (Patrick Flynn) and I2 (Thomas Flynn) are distinct individuals in a documented parent–child relationship, not duplicate identities. It appropriately relied on the existing proof summary and relationship evidence, recognised that merge operations are reserved for confirmed same-person conclusions, and avoided an incorrect merge. The reasoning is accurate, policy-compliant, and fully supported by the project evidence.

The skill fully addressed the request by correctly declining the merge and providing a complete explanation. It explained why the merge was inappropriate, referenced the supporting proof evidence, applied the merge policy correctly, and described the consequences of merging two distinct individuals. It also offered constructive alternatives and next steps, ensuring the user understood both the decision and the available options. No important aspect of the request was omitted.

No MCP tool calls were made because this was a decline/routing scenario, not a task requiring execution. The skill correctly refused the merge request and did not invoke any tree-edit operations. Since tool usage was neither required nor appropriate, the Tool Arguments dimension does not apply.

The skill correctly identified and analysed the conflict, but this was a negative test where the expected behaviour was to decline and route to conflict-resolution. Instead, it performed the conflict-resolution task itself. Because it failed the primary objective of the test (proper routing and boundary recognition), it should receive the minimum available score rather than full or partial credit for the quality of the analysis.

The skill failed to perform the required action for this negative test. Instead of declining and routing the request to conflict resolution, it performed a full conflict analysis and proposed changes. The quality of the analysis is irrelevant to the primary grading criterion because the test was specifically evaluating boundary recognition and proper routing. Since the skill omitted the required decline and routing behaviour entirely, it should receive the lowest available score.

The MCP tool call was executed correctly. The validate_research_schema invocation used the correct projectPath, matched the expected live fixture, and completed successfully with no arguments or execution errors. The tool usage was semantically correct and consistent with the expected schema-validation workflow.

The skill correctly recognised that the request required new-record discovery, not person-evidence analysis. Rather than attempting to evaluate or link existing evidence, it appropriately declined and routed the user to search records, which is the correct skill for locating additional census, military, marriage, and other records needed to answer the question. The routing decision was accurate, well justified, and consistent with the project's research workflow.

The skill fully satisfied the requirements of the negative test. It correctly recognised that the request required record discovery rather than person-evidence analysis, explained why the task was outside its scope, outlined the appropriate search strategy, provided concrete examples of records to seek, and routed the user to search-records. The response included all essential elements of a proper decline: clear boundary recognition, explanation, and actionable next steps.

No MCP tool calls were made because this was a routing/decline scenario. The skill correctly refrained from performing person-evidence work and instead routed the user to search-records. Since tool execution was neither required nor appropriate, the Tool Arguments dimension does not apply.

The skill correctly determined that no existing tree person matched James Flynn, created an appropriate stub person (I4) with the available identifying information, and linked the assertion through a person_evidence entry. The use of probable confidence and a null match_score is appropriate for a full-text-sourced assertion without a matcher score. All actions are supported by the project state and follow the expected workflow for stub creation and linkage.

The skill was fully completed in the requested task. It checked the existing tree for a matching James Flynn, determined that no suitable person existed, created the required stub person (I4), and then created the corresponding person_evidence link. This follows the expected workflow for linking an assertion when no matching tree person exists and satisfies all required steps of the task.

The MCP tool call was used correctly. The validate_research_schema invocation included the correct projectPath, matched the expected fixture arguments, and successfully validated the schema after the creation of the stub person and associated person_evidence entry. The tool choice was appropriate for verifying the integrity of the project changes, and no argument or execution errors occurred.

The probable confidence rating is correctly calibrated to the available evidence. The identification is based on a single source introducing a previously unknown individual, with no independent corroborating records yet available. The rationale appropriately distinguishes between probable and confident, reserving the higher tier for cases supported by multiple independent sources. This reflects sound genealogical methodology and aligns with the rubric's confidence standards.

The rationale is specific, evidence-based, and clearly explains the identification decision. It cites the testator's direct statement, the explicit relationship designation ("my son James Flynn"), the high-quality nature of the source, the absence of competing candidates in the existing tree, and the rationale for creating a new stub rather than forcing a match. The explanation demonstrates sound disambiguation reasoning and provides a clear audit trail from evidence to conclusion.

The skill demonstrated strong multi-person awareness. It correctly created the person_evidence link for James Flynn (I4) and recognised that the assertion carries relationship implications beyond the primary subject. Its discussion of a_004 and the need for links to both Patrick (I1) and Thomas (I2) shows an understanding that relationship assertions often involve multiple persons and require consideration of all parties. This reflects proper application of the multi-person linking model and satisfies the requirements of this dimension.

The skill correctly executed the stub-creation workflow. It created a minimal, properly structured stub person (I4) containing only the supported information (name and gender), avoided inventing unsupported facts, and linked the new person to the assertion through a valid person_evidence entry. The use of match_score: null and probable confidence is appropriate for a full-text-sourced assertion without a matcher score. The implementation follows the expected schema requirements and correctly satisfies the stub-creation criteria.

The skill handled the score discipline correctly. Because a_005 was full-text-sourced and lacked a record_persona_id, a same_person score was unavailable, making match_score: null the correct value. The confidence assessment was based on the qualitative evidence: the will directly names James Flynn as Thomas's son, providing strong identity evidence, while the absence of independent corroborating sources appropriately prevented a confident rating. The resulting probable confidence reflects sound evidence weighting and proper separation of scoring from correlation analysis.
